This guide walks you through the basics of data visualization using Matplotlib with simple explanations, code examples, and outputs.Install Matplotlib using pip:Import it in your Python script:Ideal for showing trends over time or continuous data.Common in time series analysis, stock prices, temperature changes, or performance metrics.Best for comparing discrete categories.Useful in survey results, sales by region, frequency of items, or task completion status.Great for showing proportional data or percentage breakdowns.Common in market share, budget allocation, or resource distribution.Used to show relationships or correlations between two variables.Helpful in regression analysis, outlier detection, or distribution of data points.Ideal for showing the distribution of a dataset.Common in exam scores, age distribution, or frequency of values.Add : Add : , Add : A line plot showing values increasing across X-axis points. A bar chart comparing four categories (A, B, C, D) with their respective values.Useful for comparing multiple visualizations side-by-side.Great for dashboards or reports where you want to show different perspectives of the same data.Here is the with four plots:: Line Plot – Shows trend across X-axis.: Bar Chart – Compares categories A, B, C, D.: Pie Chart – Displays fruit distribution percentages.: Scatter Plot – Highlights relationships between two variables.Save plots for presentations, reports, or sharing with others.

item.title: 📚 Reading Data from Various Data Sources Using Pandas: A Beginner’s Guide
item.author: likhitha manikonda
item.content:encoded:
makes it incredibly easy to read data from different sources like CSV, Excel, JSON, SQL databases, HTML tables, and even clipboard. This guide walks you through each method with simple examples and clear outputs.First, install Pandas if you haven’t already:Then import it in your Python script:CSV (Comma-Separated Values) is one of the most common formats.📝 : loads the CSV file into a DataFrame. shows the first 5 rows. Name Age City 0 Alice 25 New York 1 Bob 30 Paris 2 Charlie 22 Chicago 📝 : loads data from an Excel file. You can specify the sheet name. Name Age City 0 Alice 25 New York 1 Bob 30 Paris 2 Charlie 22 Chicago 📝 : reads tables from a webpage. It returns a list of DataFrames.📝 : loads structured JSON data into a DataFrame.
🗃️ Reading from SQL Databases 📝 : Connect to a SQLite database and run SQL queries directly into Pandas.📝 : reads tabular data copied to your clipboard (e.g., from Excel or a webpage).
📄 Reading Text Files with Custom Separators 📝 : Use for tab-separated files or change it to any delimiter like , , etc.Always inspect your data with , , and .Use to see all column names.Handle missing data with or .
✍️ Writing Data to Various File Formats Using Pandas Once you've manipulated your data, you’ll often want to save it. Pandas makes this easy with built-in functions to export data to formats like CSV, Excel, JSON, SQL, and more.📝 : Saves the DataFrame to a CSV file. prevents writing row numbers.: A file named containing:Name,Age,City Alice,25,New York Bob,30,Paris Charlie,22,Chicago
✅ Writing to an Excel File 📝 : Saves the DataFrame to an Excel file. You can name the sheet and skip the index.📝 : Saves the DataFrame as a JSON file. makes each row a JSON object. writes each object on a new line.
✅ Writing to a SQL Database 📝 : Saves the DataFrame to a table named in the SQLite database. overwrites the table if it already exists.📝 : Copies the DataFrame to your clipboard so you can paste it into Excel or a document.
✅ Writing to a Text File with Custom Separator 📝 : Saves the DataFrame as a tab-separated text file.Name Age City Alice 25 New York Bob 30 Paris Charlie 22 Chicago

item.title: Why Is Pune the Best Place to Learn SAP for a Thriving Career?
item.author: doit
item.content:encoded: When it comes to professional growth in the field of enterprise software, few courses hold as much value as an SAP certification. Pune, known for its IT parks, educational excellence, and professional training centers, has quickly become one of the most preferred cities to enroll in an SAP course in Pune. From offering specialized domain modules to providing hands on industry exposure, Pune’s SAP training ecosystem forms a strong foundation for anyone looking to master enterprise resource planning.Understanding the Power of SAP SAP (Systems, Applications, and Products in Data Processing) is the world’s leading ERP software used by more than 400,000 businesses globally. It integrates every part of a company’s operation from financial accounting to supply chain management—into one unified platform. The SAP course in Pune helps students, professionals, and corporate teams understand how to configure and manage these complex systems effectively. With Pune being an educational hub, many top institutes provide industry‑oriented SAP training that bridges the gap between theory and practical use. Learners gain expertise in modules like SAP FICO, SAP MM, SAP SD, SAP HCM, SAP ABAP, and SAP S/4HANA, all designed to enhance their job readiness for high‑paying positions.What Makes Pune a Preferred City for SAP Training? Pune’s vibrant IT ecosystem and academic infrastructure attract both learners and enterprises toward SAP training. The SAP course in Pune is designed to meet international standards, often delivered by certified trainers with multiple years of SAP project experience. Moreover, the city’s cost‑effective training options make it affordable compared to other metro cities. Many professionals choose Pune because it hosts the offices of multinational companies like Infosys, Tech Mahindra, IBM, and TCS—all of which have ongoing SAP implementation projects. Hence, enrolling in an SAP course in Pune allows easy access to live case studies, company internships, and recruitment opportunities.Key Features of a Quality SAP Course A well‑structured SAP course in Pune focuses equally on conceptual understanding and real‑time practice. Key features often include: Step‑by‑step modular training (functional and technical) Hands‑on lab sessions using authentic SAP environments Project work and simulation of real organizational use cases Guidance for SAP certification exams Placement support with MNC tie‑ups Access to updated SAP S/4HANA tools This practical approach ensures that learners develop a deep technical understanding and can apply SAP solutions professionally across industries.Common Questions About SAP Training in PuneWho can join an SAP course in Pune? Anyone with basic computer knowledge or experience in business domains such as finance, supply chain, or human resources can join. Fresh graduates and working professionals both benefit.How long does it take to complete the training? Typically, a full‑time SAP course in Pune lasts between 2 to 6 months, depending on the chosen module and training pace.Is prior programming experience required? Not necessarily. For functional modules like SAP FICO or SAP HR, programming isn’t mandatory. However, for technical modules like SAP ABAP, some coding knowledge helps.Do institutes offer placement assistance? Yes. Most reputed institutes hosting an SAP course in Pune provide 100% placement assistance with resume building, interview preparation, and connections to hiring partners.Can I opt for online SAP training? Absolutely. Many training centers now provide live online SAP classes, combining flexibility with direct trainer interaction and real software access.Advantages of Enrolling in an SAP Course in Pune Direct exposure to real‑time SAP project environments. Availability of multiple certified trainers with domain expertise. Affordable fee structures compared to larger metros. Excellent placement record with top IT and manufacturing companies. Enhanced networking with Pune’s rapidly growing professional community. Comprehensive syllabus covering both foundation and advanced topics. Improved job opportunities in both domestic and global markets. Access to flexible learning modes weekend, online, and classroom sessions.Disadvantages to Consider The course can be intensive, demanding consistent effort and time. Some modules have steep learning curves, especially for beginners. Not all training centers maintain standard quality, so research is critical. SAP certification exams can be costly, especially at the global level. Requires strong analytical and business understanding for best outcomes. Understanding both sides helps learners prepare realistically before enrolling in an SAP course in Pune.Why SAP Certification Adds Value Earning an SAP certification is more than a credential—it’s a validation of your technical and business skills. A well‑structured SAP course in Pune ensures that learners can confidently sit for global certification exams administered by SAP SE. With this credential, professionals find better job offers, promotions, and salary hikes. SAP‑certified experts are in great demand across industries like finance, logistics, manufacturing, and IT automation. Even consultants or freelancers can leverage their certification for global work opportunities. Since Pune has a strong base of companies implementing SAP across domains, certified professionals often get placed right after completing their course.Career Opportunities After Completing SAP Training Graduating from an SAP course in Pune opens a variety of career paths. Depending on your chosen specialization, you can pursue the following roles: SAP Functional Consultant (e.g., FICO, MM, SD) SAP Technical Consultant (e.g., ABAP Developer) SAP HANA Specialist SAP Project Manager or Implementation Lead SAP Support Engineer These roles offer lucrative salaries and global mobility, especially in technology‑driven industries adopting digital transformation.How to Choose the Right SAP Institute in Pune While searching for a reliable SAP course in Pune, consider these factors before enrolling: Accreditation: Always ensure the institute is SAP‑authorized or tied to certified trainers. Trainer Expertise: Seek professionals with real-world implementation experience. Practical Exposure: Ensure the course includes live project work. Infrastructure: Look for updated SAP servers and lab environments. Placement Record: Verify placement statistics and alumni reviews. Flexibility: Choose institutes that offer weekend or online options for working professionals. These checkpoints guarantee value for money and better employability after completion.Future Scope of SAP Professionals As global companies continue digital transformation, SAP systems remain at the heart of enterprise operations. Industries now prefer integrated ERP solutions like SAP S/4HANA that provide automation and analytics combined. Completing an SAP course in Pune positions candidates ahead in this evolving job market. With SAP’s adoption expanding across cloud, AI, and IoT domains, certified professionals can expect abundant opportunities in the next decade. Pune being both an IT and manufacturing center acts as a launchpad for such long term career prospects.Final Thoughts Whether you’re a student, a job seeker, or a working executive looking to upgrade your skills, an SAP course in Pune can be the perfect stepping stone. Its blend of expert faculty, affordable learning options, and career driven curriculum makes it one of the best choices in India for mastering SAP technology. By enrolling in a top‑rated SAP training institute in Pune, you invest in skill development that aligns with global business requirements and future industry trends.

item.title: Why Your Python Class Variables Aren’t Behaving the Way You Think | Lumir S Vinod
item.author: Lumir S Vinod
item.content:encoded: If you're a beginner in the world of object-oriented programming, you've probably faced the same doubt I did: class variables vs instance variables. Wait, wait, don't stop reading! I'm not here to dump theory like the big websites do or overload you with definitions. I want to clarify one specific concept about class and instance variables that has also confused me. A class variable is a variable defined in the class but outside the constructor or any methods. Every object shares the same memory for this variable, so its value remains the same for all objects. An instance variable, on the other hand, is an attribute defined inside methods or the constructor. Each object gets its own copy of it. Take a look at the little program I wrote:class Car: base_price = 2000 def __init__(self, brand, model, year): self.brand = brand self.year = year car1 = Car("apple", "icar, 2025) print(car1) print(Car.base_price) print(Car.base_price)Here's what I expected: when we print Car.base_price, it should show 2000. Then, when I changed base_price from 2000 to 20000, since class variables share memory space, I assumed the change would apply to all objects, even when accessed through the class name.But that's not what happened. print(Car.base_price) still showed 2000, while print(car1.base_price) showed 20000. What the heck is going on? Here's what's really happening: when we access a class variable using an object, Python first checks whether that object already has an instance variable with the same name. If it doesn't, it looks up to the class and uses the class variable. But when we assign a value to that variable through the object, Python doesn't modify the class variable. Instead, it quietly creates a new instance variable inside that object and stores the new value there. The original class variable remains unchanged. That's why car1.base_price shows 20000 (it's now using the instance variable), while Car.base_price still shows 2000 (the class variable stayed the same). So the confusion comes from Python creating a new instance variable on that object without changing the class variable. When you assign through the object. It looks like you're changing the class variable, but you're actually just creating a brand-new variable that belongs only to that object.If you want to change the value of a class variable, use the ClassName.classvariable syntax.

item.title: Pandas Series – Part 3: The Power of groupby()
item.author: Satyam Gupta
item.content:encoded: This is Part 3 of the Pandas Series, where we explore common Pandas gotchas that can level up your interview answers and your daily data work.Imagine you're in a FAANG interview. The hiring manager says, "We have a petabyte-scale dataset of user transactions. I need you to generate a daily summary report showing, for each country, the total revenue, the average order value, and the number of unique customers who made a purchase. How would you approach this with Pandas?"This is the quintessential problem. Your ability to answer it cleanly and efficiently is a massive signal of your skill level.The Core Concept: Split-Apply-Combine This is the mental model for any groupby operation. It's a three-step process: The data is broken into smaller groups based on the criteria you specify (e.g., all rows for 'USA', all rows for 'India', etc.). A function is applied to each of these smaller groups independently (e.g., , , ). The results from each group are collected and combined into a new DataFrame or Series.From Basic Aggregations to .agg()The simplest groupby is applying one function to one column: df.groupby('country')['revenue'].sum()But what if you need the sum, mean, AND count? You could run the code three times, but that's inefficient. The answer is the method. Multiple functions on one column df.groupby('country')['revenue'].agg(['sum', 'mean', 'count']) Different functions on different columns. This is where it gets powerful. You use a dictionary to specify which function to apply to which column.summary = df.groupby('country').agg( {'revenue': 'sum', # Sum of the revenue column 'order_id': 'count', # Count of all orders 'customer_id': 'nunique'} # Count of unique customers ) Level 3: Named Aggregations: The dictionary method is great, but the column names in the output can be messy. The modern, preferred, and most readable method is Named Aggregation. It lets you control the output column names directly.summary = df.groupby('country').agg( total_revenue=('revenue', 'sum'), number_of_orders=('order_id', 'count'), unique_customers=('customer_id', 'nunique') ) This syntax, new_column_name=('source_column', 'function'), is clean, explicit, and produces a perfectly formatted DataFrame. This is what interviewers love to see. ⚡ For petabyte-scale data, this logic translates directly to PySpark’s groupBy().agg() — same concept, distributed execution.And that’s a wrap for Part 3 of the Pandas Mastery Series 🎯 Next up: Should we dive into .apply() magic or merging/joining DataFrames next? Drop your pick below 👇

item.title: Automated Deployment Bash Script: Deploying a Flask App to AWS
item.author: Hyelngtil Isaac
item.content:encoded:
Hey DevOps folks! 👋 I've just wrapped up the DevOps Intern Stage 1 Task from HNG13, inspired by that dev.to challenge post. The mission? Build a single, robust Bash script to automate deploying a Dockerized app to a remote Linux server. I nailed it by deploying to an AWS EC2 instance with a simple Flask app that displays a success message and server time. This setup showcases real-world automation, idempotency, and reliability in DevOps workflows.In this article, I'll share my script, explain the process, and how it all came together. Everything's based on my actual project files, feel free to check them out and adapt!The script () handles everything in one executable file:Collect and validate user inputs (Git repo, PAT, branch, SSH details, app port).Clone or update the repo.Test SSH and prepare the remote env (install Docker, Compose, Nginx).Transfer files via rsync.Deploy the app (build/run containers idempotently).Set up Nginx reverse proxy.Validate with health checks and curls.Log everything, handle errors, and support cleanup with .I used AWS EC2 (Ubuntu 22.04) as the remote server. My app is a basic Flask site in , Dockerized via . Repo includes and a detailed .AWS EC2 instance (e.g., t2.micro Ubuntu) with SSH key access. Open security group ports: 22 (SSH), 80 (HTTP), 8080 (app, direct testing).Git repo with the app files: , , .Local machine with Git, SSH, rsync.PAT for GitHub repo access.Here's a peek at the app files for context: (Flask app serving HTML):pip requirements.txt
The Bash Script: This is the heart of it; POSIX-compliant, executable, and fully featured. Run first. pipefail
+%Y%m%d_%H%M%S log | ERR
read_input value log 1 read_input read_input PAT&21read_input read_input read_input read_input clone_repo .gitREPO_NAME
+x log 2 git fetch origin 2 git checkout 2 git pull origin 2log git clone 22 log verify_docker_configlog 0 log 3
test_ssh
log ssh 10 &>/dev/nulllog 0 log 4
setup_remote_environment
log ssh log deploy_application
log
ssh bash log transfer_files
log ssh 9
log 10
rsync 11
log configure_nginx
log ssh bash log
validate_deployment
log ssh log 6 ssh log 6 3 i 1 curl /dev/nulllog log 8i log
cleanup
log
ssh bash docker ps docker ps log cleanup 0 main log log log log
clone_repo verify_docker_config 12 test_ssh setup_remote_environment transfer_files deploy_application configure_nginx validate_deployment
log
main
How It Works: Step-by-Step : Secure prompts (PAT hidden), with defaults and validation.: Uses GIT_ASKPASS to handle PAT without exposure; pulls if exists.: Ensures is present.: Quick connectivity check with timeout.: Installs Docker/Compose/Nginx if missing, starts services.: Rsync for efficient, excluding unnecessary files.: Idempotent stops/removes old containers, builds/runs new ones (uses since no compose file).: Dynamic config proxies 80 to app port (8080 here).: Checks container status, curls the endpoint with retries.: Timestamped logs; tears down everything.: Used Ubuntu 22.04 AMI, attached SSH key, configured security groups.: SSH user (ubuntu) needs sudo; script handles Docker group add.: Ran locally, deployed to EC2—browser hit the IP showed the success page with timestamp. Re-runs worked without issues thanks to idempotency.: No SSL yet (add Certbot for prod); ensure key is 400 perms.Secure handling of secrets (like PAT) is crucial GIT_ASKPASS was a game-changer.Idempotency via stop/rm commands prevents redeploy failures.Remote exec with heredocs keeps things clean but watch for quoting.Logging + traps make debugging remote issues easier.What's your go-to automation trick in Bash? Drop it in the comments! 🚀

That’s where Bizmodo’s Restaurant POS (Point of Sale) System comes in. Designed for the fast-paced hospitality industry in the UAE, this all-in-one solution empowers restaurant owners to manage orders, billing, and inventory effortlessly while enhancing the overall customer experience. What Is a Restaurant POS System? A Restaurant POS system is a modern tool that replaces traditional cash registers and manual order-taking methods. It automates key processes like order management, billing, and reporting, enabling restaurants to operate smoothly and efficiently. With advanced features tailored for dine-in, takeaway, and delivery, Bizmodo’s POS system ensures your restaurant stays ahead of the curve. Key Features of Bizmodo’s Restaurant POS Fast and Accurate Billing Process orders quickly and eliminate billing errors with an intuitive interface that supports multiple payment methods — including cash, card, and digital wallets. Real-Time Inventory Tracking Keep track of stock levels, ingredient usage, and wastage in real time. Automatic alerts notify you when supplies are running low, preventing disruptions in your kitchen. Table and Order Management Simplify dine-in management with a visual table layout that lets staff monitor order progress and table status at a glance. Multi-Branch Control Manage multiple restaurant locations from a single dashboard. Monitor performance, sales, and reports for each branch without being physically present. Cloud-Based Access Access your restaurant’s data anytime, anywhere. Whether you’re at home or traveling, the cloud-based system keeps you in control of your business. Customer Relationship Management (CRM) Build loyalty with customer data insights, personalized promotions, and targeted marketing campaigns that encourage repeat visits. Why Choose Bizmodo’s Restaurant POS System? • Tailored for UAE Restaurants: The software is fully localized, supporting multiple currencies, tax rules, and regional preferences. • User-Friendly Interface: Easy to learn and use, even for new staff members. • Scalable for All Sizes: Ideal for cafés, fine-dining restaurants, fast-food chains, and food trucks. • Comprehensive Support: Bizmodo offers training, updates, and round-the-clock technical assistance. Benefits of Using a POS System in Restaurants A restaurant POS is not just a billing tool — it’s a business management solution. Here’s how it transforms your operations: • Saves Time: Reduces manual errors and accelerates order processing. • Enhances Accuracy: Automates tax calculations and inventory tracking. • Improves Decision-Making: Generates detailed sales reports and analytics for smarter business choices. • Boosts Profitability: Minimizes waste, optimizes staff efficiency, and increases customer satisfaction. Final Thoughts Bizmodo’s Restaurant POS system is more than just software — it’s your digital partner for success. With seamless integration, real-time insights, and an intuitive design, it helps restaurants across the UAE deliver better service and grow faster. Upgrade your restaurant today with Bizmodo’s Restaurant POS and experience the power of smart business management. 👉 Learn more at: https://bizmodo.ae/restaurant-pos/

item.title: Day 24 of 100 days dsa coding challenge
item.author: Manasi Patil
item.content:encoded:
Taking on a new challenge: solving GeeksforGeeks POTD daily and sharing my solutions! 💻🔥 The goal: sharpen problem-solving skills, level up coding, and learn something new every day. Follow my journey! 🚀Minimum Steps to Halve SumDifficulty: Medium Accuracy: 62.81%Given an array arr[], find the minimum number of operations required to make the sum of its elements less than or equal to half of the original sum. In one operation, you may replace any element with half of its value (with floating-point precision). Input: arr[] = [8, 6, 2] Explanation: Initial sum = (8 + 6 + 2) = 16, half = 8 Halve 8 → arr[] = [4, 6, 2], sum = 12 (still 12 > 8) Halve 6 → arr[] = [4, 3, 2], sum = 9 (still 9 > 8) Halve 2 → arr[] = [4, 3, 1], sum = 8. Input: arr[] = [9, 1, 2] Explanation: Initial sum = 12, half = 6 Halve 9 → arr[] = [4.5, 1, 2], sum = 7.5 (still > 6) Halve 4.5 → arr[] = [2.25, 1, 2], sum = 5.25 ≤ 6 1 ≤ arr.size() ≤ 105class Solution: def minOperations(self, arr): target = total / 2 heapq.heapify(heap) while total - reduced > target: val = -heapq.heappop(heap) half = val / 2 reduced += half heapq.heappush(heap, -half) ops += 1
return ops

item.title: A Beginner's Guide to Data Analysis with Python: Using Pandas and NumPy
item.author: Saint
item.content:encoded: Data is everywhere, and Python is the go-to language for making sense of it all. Its simple syntax and powerful libraries have made it a favourite among data scientists. If you're looking to get started in data analysis, you've come to the right place. This guide will walk you through a complete beginner's project using Python's two most essential data analysis libraries: NumPy and Pandas.The Power Duo: NumPy and PandasThink of NumPy and Pandas as the foundational tools for data analysis in Python. NumPy (Numerical Python): This is the engine for numerical computing. Its core feature is the powerful n-dimensional array (ndarray), which allows for high-speed mathematical operations on large datasets. This is achieved through vectorisation, which applies operations to entire arrays simultaneously instead of looping through elements one by one. Pandas: Built on top of NumPy, Pandas is your tool for data manipulation and analysis. It introduces the DataFrame, a two-dimensional table similar to a spreadsheet, which makes working with structured data intuitive and robust.Let's Get Analysing the Iris DatasetThe best way to learn is through hands-on experience. We'll use the classic Iris flower dataset, which is ideal for beginners due to its small size, cleanliness, and ease of understanding. It contains measurements for 150 iris flowers from three different species. Step 1: Set Up and Load the DataFirst, let's import our libraries and load the dataset directly from its URL into a Pandas DataFrame. The function is powerful enough to handle this in one step.Step 2: Get a First Look at Your DataNow that the data is loaded, let's perform a quick inspection to understand its structure. Pandas has some handy functions for this. : Shows the first five rows.: Shows the number of rows and columns.: Gives a summary of columns, data types, and non-null values.: Provides descriptive statistics for numerical columns.From this, we can confirm we have 150 rows and five columns, with no missing data!Step 3: Ask Questions and AnalyseThis is where we start "slicing and dicing" the data to find insights.Question 1: "Show me only the 'Iris-setosa' flowers."We can use boolean indexing to filter the DataFrame based on a condition.Question 2: "What is the average sepal length for each species?"The method is perfect for this. It splits the data by category (such as 'species'), applies a function (like ), and combines the results.This simple command provides a powerful summary, highlighting apparent differences in the average measurements between the species.A chart is often better than a table. Let's create a bar chart to compare the average petal width for each species. Pandas can plot directly from our grouped data.This visualisation instantly tells a story: Iris-virginica has, on average, a much broader petal than Iris-setosa.Congratulations! You've just completed an end-to-end data analysis project. You loaded, analysed, and visualised a dataset to uncover insights.This is just the beginning. The best way to get better is to practice. Find another dataset and try applying these same steps. What questions can you ask? What stories can you tell with the data analysis?

item.title: Build Your Own Forum with FastAPI: Step 5 - Editing Posts
item.author: Leapcell
item.content:encoded:
In the previous article, we built a user system for our forum. Now, users can register, log in, and publish posts under their own identity.However, once a post is published, it currently cannot be modified. What if you spot an error or want to add more content?In this article, we will implement a new feature: allowing users to edit the posts they have created.
Step 1: Create the Post Edit Page First, we need a new HTML page where users can modify the title and content of their posts. This page will be very similar to the new post form, but it will come pre-filled with the post's existing data.In the folder, create a new file named .Edit Post - My FastAPI ForumEdit Post{{ post.content }}Update PostCancelThe core of this template is a form with two input fields: and . We use Jinja2's and syntax to pre-populate the form with the post data passed from the backend.
Step 2: Add API Routes for Editing Next, we need to add two new routes in to handle the editing logic. Open the file and add the following code: (Adding new routes)Here, we've added two routes:GET /posts/{post_id}/edit: This route finds a post in the database based on the from the URL. It then verifies that the matches the . If the validation passes, it renders the template, passing the queried object to it.POST /posts/{post_id}/edit: This route receives the new and from the form and updates the attributes of the object. This operation also validates the login status and post ownership.
Step 3: Add an Entry Point on the Main Page The routes are ready. Next, we need to add an entry point for posts so that users can access the edit page.Modify the file. (Updated)... (File header and stylesheet remain unchanged) ...
... (Header and new post form sections remain unchanged) ...
Post List
{% for post in posts %} {{ post.title }}{{ post.content }}Author: {{ post.owner.username if post.owner else 'Unknown' }}
{% if current_user and post.owner_id == current_user.id %} Edit {% endif %}
{% endfor %} We've added a Jinja2 conditional statement below the post information:{% if current_user and post.owner_id == current_user.id %}If the current user is logged in ( exists) and the of the post matches the of the current user, an "Edit" link pointing to /posts/{{ post.id }}/edit will be displayed.This way, users will only see the edit button on their own posts and cannot edit posts belonging to others.Restart your uvicorn server:Visit and log in.Find a post you previously published, and you will see an "Edit" link below it. For posts by other users, you will not see the "Edit" link.Click "Edit," and the page will redirect to the edit form, pre-filled with the post's original title and content.Modify the content and click "Update Post." The page will redirect back to the homepage, where you will see that the post content has been successfully updated.
Deploying the Project Online Just like in the first tutorial, you can deploy the results of this step online to let your friends experience the changes and progress of your project.A simple deployment solution is to use Leapcell.If you have deployed before, simply push the code to your Git repository, and Leapcell will automatically redeploy the latest code for you.If you haven't used Leapcell's deployment service before, you can refer to the tutorial in this article.Now that the forum has an editing feature, it has become more complete.Besides posting, interaction is essential for a forum. When users see an interesting (or controversial) post, they will want to share their opinions below it.In the next article, we will add an interactive feature to the forum: implementing comments and replies for posts, allowing users to have discussions around them.

item.title: はじめまして、濱元です。フリーランスエンジニアとしての挑戦
item.author: Hamamoto Kazuma
item.content:encoded: 長野県出身、現在はフリーランスエンジニアとして活動を始めました。 主に を中心に、データレイク構築・生成AIを活用した新規開発・R&Dに取り組んでいます。前職では、AWS環境での Glue・Athena・Lambda・API Gateway などを活用したデータ基盤の構築や、音声応答やドキュメント整形などの を推進。 社内の業務効率化や新規事業開発の一翼を担ってきました。現在は、これまで培ってきた と の知見を活かし、 企業の課題に対して柔軟な技術提案・実装支援を行うことを目指しています。Python（FastAPI, Flask, Django など）AWS（Glue, Athena, Lambda, API Gateway, CloudFormation）2026年ごろには関東圏（埼玉・神奈川・千葉・東京）へ拠点を移し、 フルリモートでの開発・R&D活動を広げていく予定です。 個人としても、「AI × 医療・ヘルスケア」領域の可能性を深く探っていきたいと考えています。もし技術的なテーマやプロジェクト事例に興味を持ってもらえたら、ぜひ気軽にコメントや連絡をください。 ゆるく話せるエンジニア仲間も募集中です。

item.title: an introduction to Dask
item.author: seng
item.content:encoded: Dask is a large and useful Python library that is regularly used for parallel and distributed computing. If your Python code is written using a loop structure, it can be parallelized with Dask.Firstly, it can help you construct customized pipelines and workflows, process a massive number of tasks, and execute them easily.Secondly, Dask DataFrames provide the facilities for larger-than-memory execution, parallel execution, and distributed computation.Lastly, Dask ML facilitates the use of scikit-learn and other machine learning libraries for parallel and distributed environments.By the way, Dask Bags, which parallelize Python lists simply, are commonly used to process text or raw Python objects.You can install Dask in the following way: pip install dask

item.title: Title: Barry Silbert Returns to Grayscale as Chairman Amid IPO Push
item.author: Insights YRS
item.content:encoded: Title: Barry Silbert Returns to Grayscale as Chairman Amid IPO Push Digital Currency Group (DCG) has announced that Barry Silbert, the founder of DCG, has been re-appointed as chairman of the board at asset management firm Grayscale. Silbert, who also owns Grayscale, resigned in December 2023 after the company was hit with a lawsuit from the New York Attorney General's office. The lawsuit accused Silbert and DCG of misleading investors and hiding financial losses totaling more than $1 billion. Despite the ongoing legal back-and-forth with the New York Attorney General's office, Grayscale filed a confidential submission for an initial public offering (IPO) last month.Silbert's Return to Grayscale:Silbert's return to Grayscale as chairman is a significant development for the company and the broader digital asset ecosystem. Silbert has a deep conviction in the company's long-term positioning and the leadership team guiding it forward. He has been a key player in the digital asset industry for over a decade and has a wealth of experience in the sector.Grayscale has been pushing for an IPO for some time now. The company filed a confidential submission for an IPO last month, despite the ongoing legal back-and-forth with the New York Attorney General's office. Grayscale has been converting its flagship Bitcoin Trust (GBTC) into an exchange-traded fund (ETF) as well as its Ethereum counterpart. It is also in the process of launching or converting several other products as ETFs.Grayscale has also made several high-ranking hires to its leadership team. The company hired a new chief operating officer, chief marketing officer, chief communications officer, and as chief human resources officer. These new hires will report to Grayscale CEO Peter Mintzberg.Silbert's return to Grayscale as chairman is a positive development for the company and the broader digital asset ecosystem. Grayscale's push for an IPO is a significant step towards mainstream adoption of digital assets. The company's new hires will bring fresh perspectives and expertise to the leadership team, which will help Grayscale navigate the challenges and opportunities in the digital asset space.

item.title: Title: A Comprehensive Review of Honor's Slim Magic V5 Foldable Smartphone
item.author: Insights YRS
item.content:encoded: Title: A Comprehensive Review of Honor's Slim Magic V5 Foldable Smartphone Honor, a leading smartphone brand, has recently released its latest flagship device, the Slim Magic V5. This foldable smartphone boasts a sleek and slim design, making it an attractive option for tech-savvy users. In this review, we will take a closer look at the device's features, performance, and user experience.The Slim Magic V5 has a premium build quality, with a durable aluminum frame and a high-quality glass display. The device's slim design makes it easy to hold and use, and the foldable screen allows for versatile viewing options. The device's weight is also well-balanced, making it comfortable to use for extended periods.The Slim Magic V5's camera is one of its standout features. The device comes equipped with a 64-megapixel primary camera, a 12-megapixel ultra-wide camera, and a 50-megapixel telephoto camera. The camera's performance is impressive, with accurate colors and sharp images. However, the device's camera bump can be a bit of a menace, as it protrudes out of the device's body.The Slim Magic V5 is powered by a high-performance Snapdragon 865 processor, making it a top-of-the-line device. The device's RAM is also impressive, with 12GB of RAM available. The device's battery life is also commendable, with a 4500mAh battery providing ample power for extended use.The Slim Magic V5's user experience is top-notch. The device runs on the latest version of Android, with a custom user interface that is easy to navigate. The device's foldable screen also provides a versatile viewing experience, making it ideal for both work and entertainment.Overall, the Slim Magic V5 is a well-designed and high-performing device. Its camera is impressive, and the device's build quality is top-notch. However, the camera bump can be a bit of a menace, and it may deter some users from purchasing the device. If you are looking for a top-of-the-line foldable smartphone with a sleek design and impressive performance, the Slim Magic V5 is definitely worth considering.

item.title: Microscopic Vision: AI Unlocks the Secrets of Surface Quality by Arvind Sundararajan
item.author: Arvind SundaraRajan
item.content:encoded:
Imagine a critical component failing prematurely, not due to design flaws, but because of subtle surface imperfections invisible to the naked eye. Traditional quality control struggles to detect these minute variations, leading to wasted materials and compromised product performance. What if AI could not only these flaws, but also predict their impact with quantifiable certainty?At its core, this involves a deep learning architecture capable of analyzing surface scans – whether from optical or tactile sensors – to predict not just roughness parameters, but also the reliability of those predictions. We achieve this by training models with multiple output "heads," one predicting the surface texture (e.g., average roughness), and another predicting the associated with that prediction. The system effectively learns to say, "The roughness is X, and I'm Y% confident in that estimate."This allows manufacturers to make informed decisions, going beyond simple pass/fail criteria. For instance, we can now identify components where the surface texture is within acceptable limits, but the prediction has high uncertainty, signaling a potentially unstable manufacturing process.
Benefits of AI-Powered Surface Analysis: Identify flawed parts earlier in the production process.Improved Product Reliability: Predict potential failures based on surface characteristics.Optimized Manufacturing Processes: Fine-tune parameters to minimize surface defects.Data-Driven Decision Making: Quantify the risk associated with each component.Automated Quality Control: Reduce reliance on subjective visual inspection. Anticipate wear and tear based on surface degradation.Implementation Challenge: Training requires diverse datasets reflecting various measurement instruments. A practical tip is to simulate data augmentations that mimic different sensor characteristics, creating a more robust and generalizable model.Think of it like a weather forecast. Knowing there's a 30% chance of rain is useful, but knowing the forecaster is makes it actionable. If the confidence is low, you might still grab an umbrella. Similarly, in manufacturing, low confidence in surface quality assessments can trigger further inspection or process adjustments.The future of manufacturing relies on precise and reliable measurements. By embracing AI to model uncertainty, we're not just detecting surface flaws; we're building a new paradigm of predictive quality control, moving from reactive failure analysis to proactive defect prevention. Imagine this applied to personalized medicine, where the texture of a bio-implant surface dictates its integration success. The potential is vast. surface roughness, 3D imaging, texture analysis, defect detection, anomaly detection, image segmentation, neural networks, convolutional neural networks (CNN), transfer learning, fine-tuning, data augmentation, manufacturing defects, quality assurance, process optimization, predictive maintenance, optical metrology, atomic force microscopy (AFM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), metrology software, industrial automation

item.title: The Library Method: Understanding Context Managers
item.author: Aaron Rose
item.content:encoded:
Context managers aren't magic - they're Python's way of automating try/finally for guaranteed cleanupTimothy enters the library and heads to Margaret's desk. "Margaret, I've been using statements, but I don't understand how they work."Opening resource Using resource Closing resource "It prints in that order - but how does Python guarantee 'Closing resource' prints even if there's an error?" "Let's use the method to understand it." "Here's what's happening:"What a context manager does:An object with two special methods: and When you write , Python does four things:
Calls → runs setup code, returns a valueCalls → runs cleanup code The key: always runs, even if there's an errorIt's like an automatic try/finally block built into the syntax "So guarantees the cleanup?" "Yes. Let's prove it with Pascal."Opening resource Using resource Closing resource opens the Pascal compilerOpening resource Using resource Closing resource "See? Three clear phases: enter, use, exit. In production Pascal code, you'd wrap this in a try-finally block to guarantee the exit always runs, even with errors. But the pattern is the same - structured resource management with guaranteed cleanup." "Let me write what is doing behind the scenes."Opening resource Using resource Closing resource "Same output - so is just wrapping it in try/finally." "Now look at the original code again."Opening resource Using resource Closing resource "So is automatic resource management using try/finally." "Exactly. The method always runs, guaranteeing cleanup. That's why files close automatically, locks release, connections close - the cleanup is guaranteed." All three implementations produced identical output showing guaranteed cleanup.Context managers have (setup) and (cleanup) methods statement automatically wraps code in try/finally always runs, even if there's an errorThis guarantees resource cleanup - Setup, use, cleanup with guarantee - try/finally showing the guarantee - Explicit version of what does - automates the try/finally pattern Context managers aren't magic - they're Python's way of automating try/finally for guaranteed cleanup. Whether you use or explicit try/finally, you now understand the mechanism.Next in The Library Method: Understanding @property - How do methods act like attributes?

item.title: List and Tuples are Compound Data types
item.author: Onaolapo-11
item.content:encoded: Day 66 [October 24, 2025]I need to buckle down, as I'm still lagging on day day 3 & 4 goals, "Day 3-4: Control structures (if-else, loops)", as well as day 5 (and 6) goals, "Day 5-6: Functions and modules", and Day 7 target (exercises) (Meta AI, personal communication, August 8, 2025). If I haven't covered this, I can't make progress on day 8 - 63 goals.As extracted from the 'Python for Software Development' textbook by Halvorsen (n.d.):The New Age of Programming ✅Interpreted vs. Compiled ✅Python Packages for Science and Numerical Computations ✅Using Python Libraries, Packages and Modules✅Creating Functions in Python - IntroductionFunctions with multiple return valuesCreating Classes in PythonPython for Data Science, AI & Development Course (IBM) (Santarcangelo, n.d.):Module 2: Python Data StructuresCalled , so called because multiple values of different data types can be held in them, also allowing the organization and management of data collection (see also Google, 2025) List and Tuples are Compound Data types. Another example are Dictionaries.

item.title: Giampaolo Rodola: Wheels for free-threaded Python now available in psutil
item.content:encoded: With the release of , wheels for free-threaded Python are now available. This milestone was achieved largely through a , as several internal refactorings to the C code were required to make it possible (see issue #2565). Many of these changes were contributed by Lysandros Nikolaou. Thanks to him for the effort and for bearing with me in code reviews! ;-)What is free-threaded Python?Free-threaded Python (available since ) refers to Python builds that are compiled with the GIL (Global Interpreter Lock) disabled, allowing true parallel execution of Python bytecodes across multiple threads. This is particularly beneficial for , as it enables better utilization of multi-core processors.The state of free-threaded wheelsAccording to Hugo van Kemenade's free-threaded wheels tracker, the adoption of free-threaded wheels among the top 360 most-downloaded PyPI packages with C extensions is still limited. Only 128 out of these 360 packages provide wheels compiled for free-threaded Python, meaning they can run on Python builds with the GIL disabled. This shows that, while progress has been made, most popular packages with C extensions still do not offer ready-made wheels for free-threaded Python.When a library author provides a wheel, users can install a pre-compiled binary package without having to build it from source. This is especially important for packages with , like psutil, which is largely written in C. Such packages often have complex build requirements and require installing a C compiler. On Windows, that means installing or the Build Tools, which can take several gigabytes and a setup effort. Providing wheels spare users from this hassle, makes installation far simpler, and is effectively of that package. You basically and you're done.What it means for library authorsCurrently, universal wheels for free-threaded Python do not exist. Each wheel must be built specifically for a Python version. Right now authors must create separate wheels for Python 3.13 and 3.14. Which means distributing of files already:psutil-7.1.2-cp313-cp313t-macosx_10_13_x86_64.whl psutil-7.1.2-cp313-cp313t-macosx_11_0_arm64.whl psutil-7.1.2-cp313-cp313t-manylinux2010_x86_64.manylinux_2_12_x86_64.manylinux_2_28_x86_64.whl psutil-7.1.2-cp313-cp313t-manylinux2014_aarch64.manylinux_2_17_aarch64.manylinux_2_28_aarch64.whl psutil-7.1.2-cp313-cp313t-win_amd64.whl psutil-7.1.2-cp313-cp313t-win_arm64.whl psutil-7.1.2-cp314-cp314t-macosx_10_15_x86_64.whl psutil-7.1.2-cp314-cp314t-macosx_11_0_arm64.whl psutil-7.1.2-cp314-cp314t-manylinux2010_x86_64.manylinux_2_12_x86_64.manylinux_2_28_x86_64.whl psutil-7.1.2-cp314-cp314t-manylinux2014_aarch64.manylinux_2_17_aarch64.manylinux_2_28_aarch64.whl psutil-7.1.2-cp314-cp314t-win_amd64.whl psutil-7.1.2-cp314-cp314t-win_arm64.whl This also multiplies CI jobs and slows down the test matrix (see build.yml). A would greatly reduce this overhead, allowing a single wheel to support multiple Python versions and platforms. Hopefully, will simplify this process. Two competing proposals, PEP 803 and PEP 809, aim to standardize wheel naming and metadata to allow producing a single wheel that covers multiple Python versions. That would drastically reduce distribution complexity for library authors, and it's fair to say it's essential for free-threaded CPython to truly succeed.How to install free-threaded psutilYou can now install psutil for free-threaded Python directly via :pipinstallpsutil--only-binary:all: This ensures you get the without triggering a source build.

item.title: AI-Powered Cover Letter Generator
item.author: Hana Belay
item.content:encoded:
If you have been applying for different jobs, you know how time-consuming and repetitive writing cover letters can be. On top of that, trying to make sure your cover letter includes the job requirements and effectively highlights your relevant skills can feel repetitive. But what if we could automate this process?This is how I got the inspiration to build an AI-powered cover letter generator that automates this process while maintaining guidelines that make cover letters effective. In this article, I will walk you through how I built this application. handles the heavy lifting on the backend. provides smooth interactivity without the complexity of a full JavaScript framework. understand job requirements and generate tailored content. help find patterns in successful cover letters. manages a minimal client-side state. provides a rapid way of building user interfaces.
Application Flow and Demo Before diving into the technical details, let's see how the flow works. The first thing you need is to upload a resume. The application automatically extracts and processes your experience, skills, and achievements. You can upload multiple resumes and mark one as primary, a handy feature if you maintain different versions for various roles.The process is very simple afterward:If needed, refine the letter by providing feedback (e.g., or "Emphasize my leadership experience").Mark cover letters as favorite to influence future generations.Here are some screenshots!Uploading and managing resumes is central to this application. Since people often maintain different resume versions for various roles, the application allows users to upload multiple resumes and select one as their primary. Below is the Django model for handling resume uploads:The following extracts the content from a PDF using pypdfYou can also extend this service to use other parsing tools like llama-parse.
Vector Embeddings and pgvector The key to generating good cover letters is understanding both the job requirements and the person’s experience. This is where vector embeddings come into play.Vector embeddings numerically represent data as an array of numbers (vectors) and capture semantic relationships and similarities between data points. Once data is represented in numbers, semantic relationships can be found by seeing how close data points are to each other as points in vector spaces using techniques like cosine similarity.When working with embeddings, you need a way to efficiently store and query these numerical representations. While there are several approaches - from simple file storage to specialized vector databases like Pinecone or Weaviate - I wanted a solution that wouldn't complicate the infrastructure. Enter pgvector - an extension that directly adds vector similarity search capabilities to PostgreSQL. Two reasons for using pgvector in your Django project:There is no need for a separate vector database - everything stays in PostgreSQL.Simple setup with Docker and seamless integration with Django.Setting it up is straightforward with Docker:Then install pgvector python, a package that provides support for pgvector in Python:This package supports different database libraries, including Django and SQLAlchemy. To enable this extension in Django, we have to first create a migration:Then run the migrate command and that is it!Once the extension is enabled, you can define in your models to store vector embeddings:In the code snippet above, the field stores vector representations of job details. But how do we generate these embeddings? While there are multiple methods - such as using Sentence Transformers or Hugging Face's Transformers library - I chose OpenAI's model for this project due to its simplicity:
Generating Cover Letters with OpenAI Once we have our embeddings and similar examples, it's time to generate the actual cover letter. I am using OpenAI's model but you can easily configure other services if you wish to do so:While the current version gets the job done, there's always room for improvement. Some ideas for the future:Knowledge base of external documents/resources about industry guidelines.Integration with job application platforms to automatically extract job details.The complete source code is available on GitHub. Feel free to use it directly, contribute improvements, or check the implementation in detail.

item.title: Taming the Chaos: A Python Guide to Beating Race Conditions in Multithreading
item.author: Aris Georgatos
item.content:encoded:
You've heard the buzz: multithreading can dramatically improve your application's responsiveness and throughput, especially for I/O-bound tasks like web requests or file operations. You start a few threads, you watch your program fly.But then, the chaos begins.Your beautiful code starts behaving like a moody teenager, unpredictable, inconsistent, and occasionally flat-out wrong. The problem isn't your logic but it's a hidden culprit called a .What exactly is a race condition in simple terms?A race condition occurs when the outcome of your program depends on the timing of multiple threads accessing a shared resource. Imagine two people at separate ATMs trying to withdraw money from the same bank account at the exact same time. Without a proper mechanism to enforce turns, one person might read the account balance before the other person's withdrawal has been fully processed, leading to an incorrect balance (and a potential security nightmare!).Why are race conditions so dangerous?Race conditions are notoriously difficult to debug because they're non-deterministic. Your code might work perfectly 99 times, then fail catastrophically on the 100th run. The same input produces different outputs depending on thread timing, which is affected by CPU load, system scheduling, and pure chance.The good news: Python's module provides several battle-tested tools to eliminate race conditions. Each strategy solves a different coordination problem. Let's explore them one by one, starting with the most fundamental.
Strategy #1 Mutex / Locks
When a shared mutable resource (counter, list, dict, file) is being read+written by multiple threads.Here is the approach.Here is the approach. introduce serialization at the critical section, which means threads wait in line. This can become a bottleneck. If your critical section is large, you're essentially running single-threaded code with threading overhead!
Strategy #2 Condition Variables (Simplified)
When threads need to wait for a specific event without wasting CPU cycles checking repeatedly. The waiter doesn't constantly ask "Is it ready? Is it ready?" (which wastes energy). Instead, the waiter waits patiently, and the chef says "It's ready!" exactly when it's done. which makes the waiter thread sleep (uses zero CPU) until wakes it up. When is called, it doesn't just sleep, it atomically releases the lock and then sleeps. If it kept holding the lock while sleeping, the chef could never acquire it to set . This pattern is synchronized sleeping, not just sleeping.When you wish to limit concurrent access to N identical resources (connection pools, API rate limits, worker threads).When you wish to control throughput without forcing serial execution (downloading files, processing batches).When you wish to manage resource pools where multiple threads can safely work in parallel, just not ALL at once. A maintains an internal counter initialized to (the maximum concurrent accesses). When a thread calls , the counter decrements, when it calls , the counter increments. If a thread tries to acquire when the counter is 0, it blocks until another thread releases. This is implemented using OS-level synchronization primitives (like POSIX semaphores on Unix or semaphore objects on Windows) that efficiently put threads to sleep rather than busy-waiting, ensuring minimal CPU overhead.
Strategy #4 Atomic Operations An atomic operation completes in a single, indivisible step. No other thread can see it "half-done." This eliminates race conditions for simple operations without needing locks.Example #1 Python's Atomic Counter Simple counters, ID generation, or any single-value increment without complex logic. is implemented in , not Python. GIL ensures that executes Python bytecode at a time. When you call , the entire operation happens while holding the GIL, meaning no other Python thread can interrupt it. The actual increment happens in C code, which completes before releasing the GIL. The read-increment-store sequence happens at the C level, not as separate Python bytecode instructions.Example #2 Thread-Isolated Storage When each thread needs its own copy of a resource (database connections, user sessions, request context, buffers).When you create a object, you're not creating a single shared variable that all threads fight over. Instead, you're creating a special container where Python automatically gives each thread its own private copy of whatever you store in it.In a normal scenario with a shared variable, if writes and writes at the same time, they're fighting over the same memory location, meaning one will overwrite the other. But with , they're writing to completely separate memory locations that just happen to have the same name.
Wrapping Up: Choosing the Right Tool Race conditions are in multithreaded code, but with the right synchronization primitives, you can eliminate them entirely. Here's your decision tree:Start by asking yourself:"Does only ONE thread need exclusive access?" => Use a Lock "Do threads need to wait for a specific condition/event?" => Use a Condition Variable "Can N threads safely work concurrently, but not MORE than N?" => Use a Semaphore "Is this a simple operation that doesn't need coordination?" => Use Atomic Operations or Thread-Local Storage

item.title: Day 23 of 100 days dsa coding challenge
item.author: Manasi Patil
item.content:encoded: Taking on a new challenge: solving GeeksforGeeks POTD daily and sharing my solutions! 💻🔥 The goal: sharpen problem-solving skills, level up coding, and learn something new every day. Follow my journey! 🚀Difficulty: Medium Accuracy: 51.83%Given a sorted integer array arr[] and an integer k, determine if it is possible to split the array into one or more consecutive subsequences such that: Each subsequence consists of consecutive integers (each number is exactly one greater than the previous). Every subsequence has a length of at least k. Return true if such a split is possible, otherwise return false. Input: arr[] = [2, 2, 3, 3, 4, 5], k = 2 Explanation: arr can be split into three subsequence of length k - [2, 3], [2, 3], [4, 5]. Input: arr[] = [1, 1, 1, 1, 1], k = 4 Output: false Explanation: It is impossible to split arr into consecutive increasing subsequences of length 4 or more. 1 ≤ arr.size() ≤ 105 1 ≤ k ≤ arr.size() class Solution: def isPossible(self, arr, k): from collections import Counter, defaultdict end = defaultdict(int) if freq[num] == 0: freq[num] -= 1 end[num - 1] -= 1 else: for nxt in range(num + 1, num + k): valid = False freq[nxt] -= 1 return False return True

item.title: urllib – Python Standard Library for URL Handling
item.author: MrRobot
item.content:encoded: urllib – Python Standard Library for URL Handling is a built-in Python library that provides modules for working with URLs. It supports opening, reading, and parsing URLs, handling HTTP requests, encoding and decoding query strings, and working with internet resources. Since it’s part of the standard library, it’s commonly used for web scraping, downloading files, or interacting with web APIs without requiring third-party libraries. (No installation required — comes with Python.)Build a simple web scraper to extract information from websites.Download images or files from a list of URLs.Create a small URL shortener or query string builder.

item.title: Pygments – Syntax Highlighting for Code and Text in python
item.author: MrRobot
item.content:encoded: Pygments is a powerful Python library for syntax highlighting of code in various programming languages. It supports hundreds of languages and formats, making it ideal for documentation generators, static site builders, and any application that needs to display code beautifully. It can output highlighted code in HTML, LaTeX, RTF, SVG, and more formats.Build a syntax-highlighted code viewer for a web app or blog.Create a Markdown-to-HTML converter that highlights code blocks.Develop a command-line tool to generate highlighted PDFs or HTML files from source code.

item.title: 5 Pandas Labs: Master .query(), E-commerce String Cleaning, and Advanced Data Filtering with .where()
item.author: Labby
item.content:encoded:
Ready to stop struggling with messy data? Pandas is the undisputed champion for data manipulation in Python. But mastering it requires more than just reading documentation—you need hands-on practice. The LabEx Pandas learning path is designed specifically for beginners, giving you a clear roadmap to master DataFrames, data cleaning, and advanced analysis. We’ve picked five essential, quick labs that will immediately boost your confidence and practical skills. Let’s dive into the core techniques you need to become a data wizard!
The Powerful Query Method Beginner | 30 minutesIn this challenge, you will dive deep into the powerful .query() method. This method allows you to filter DataFrame rows using a concise and expressive syntax. It is a great alternative to boolean indexing, especially when dealing with complex filtering conditions. By the end of this challenge, you will have a solid grasp of the .query() method and its various use cases.
Pandas String Manipulation for E-commerce Data Beginner | 15 minutesPandas is an open-source data analysis and manipulation tool built on top of Python's powerful data and science libraries. One of the most widely used features in Pandas is its string manipulation capabilities.
DataFrame Math Operations Beginner | 15 minutesIn this challenge, we will dive deep into the heart of pandas, one of the most widely used data manipulation libraries in Python. We will explore complex mathematical operations on pandas DataFrames, manipulating data in various interesting and challenging ways. Our focus will be on performing operations that involve both element-wise computations and aggregation operations. Beginner | 20 minutesIn this challenge, you will be working with Pandas Series, mastering your skills and understanding of this essential data structure in the Pandas library.
Exploring the Where Function Beginner | 15 minutesThe Pandas library is an essential tool for data manipulation and analysis in Python. It provides robust and versatile data structures, such as DataFrame and Series, to facilitate the management of large and complex datasets.These five hands-on labs are your fast track to Pandas proficiency. They move beyond theoretical concepts, forcing you to apply core methods like , , and string manipulation on real datasets. Stop feeling overwhelmed by data wrangling. Start these interactive challenges today and transform yourself from a beginner into a confident data analyst ready to tackle any dataset thrown your way.
